root.text:
I came across this mind blowing piece of software engineering today:
<https://justine.lol/ape.html>
I found this quote to be particularly interesting:
> One of the reasons why I love working with a lot of these old unsexy technologies, is that *I want any software work I'm involved in to stand the test of time with minimal toil.* Similar to how the Super Mario Bros ROM has managed to survive all these years without needing a GitHub issue tracker.
> *I believe the best chance we have of doing that, is by gluing together the binary interfaces that've already achieved a decades-long consensus, and ignoring the APIs.* 
(emphasis mine)

I can't help but see some overlap with some of the work that <@UCUSW7WVD> has done (especially his work on removing reliance on an Operating System).

attachments.fallback: <https://twitter.com/EmilStenstrom|@EmilStenstrom>: Redbean looks amazing! It’s a multi platform binary that you can add stuff to as if it was a zip file. It is a zip file. But it’s also a crazy fast web server that can serve millions of requests against the contents of the zip file. Crazy cool engineering! <https://justine.lol/redbean/index.html>

text: This speaker's concept of symmetry as applied to language design is a powerful one, and will probably stay near the top of my mental toolbox. Ze also uses this to drive some interesting ideas about how to build nested sublanguages of increasing power (something I'm especially interested in) by progressively breaking said symmetries. Apparently there's some influence from E as well. <https://youtu.be/jm5qWioS9S0|https://youtu.be/jm5qWioS9S0>

attachments.title: "Noether: Symmetry in Programming Language Design" by Daira Hopwood

<https://discourse.codinghorror.com/t/updating-101-basic-computer-games-for-2021/7927|https://discourse.codinghorror.com/t/updating-101-basic-computer-games-for-2021/7927>

attachments.title: Updating "101 Basic Computer Games" for 2021
attachments.text: Here’s a topic to capture some thoughts on this potential project. I initially started thinking about it when I discovered this 2010 reboot: But I was really disappointed in the resulting updated code, I feel like an opportunity was missed to demonstrate better coding patterns that have emerged since the 1970s. I don’t mean super fancy, complex things, but stuff as simple as, y’know, subroutines? Like James Shore said here <https://www.jamesshore.com/v2/blog/2006/quality-with-a-name> So I as...
attachments.fallback: Coding Horror Discussion: Updating "101 Basic Computer Games" for 2021

https://discourse.codinghorror.com/t/updating-101-basic-computer-games-for-2021/7927

text: So is it just me or are most of the modern examples universally worse and completely missing the point of this type of book?

elements.text: So is it just me or are most of the modern examples universally worse and completely missing the point of this type of book?

text: For something new, I'd start with RDF. The conceptual building block is simple (subject-predicate-object), which makes it pretty flexible. They now also have SPARQL - a query language for RDF. I'd pick RDF over any relational or algebraic schema models. I think it is closest to the conceptual level of most subjects.

elements.text: For something new, I'd start with RDF. The conceptual building block is simple (subject-predicate-object), which makes it pretty flexible. They now also have SPARQL - a query language for RDF. I'd pick RDF over any relational or algebraic schema models. I think it is closest to the conceptual level of most subjects.

text: Most of them are longer in terms of LOC, they're harder to read for beginners in terms of number of concepts introduced and overall boilerplate.

elements.text: Most of them are longer in terms of LOC, they're harder to read for beginners in terms of number of concepts introduced and overall boilerplate.

text: They clean up some logic and code tricks that are hard to parse or only work in Basic, which is nice

text:
Redbean is getting all the Hacker News attention, but Justine Tunney's underlying philosophy and engineering is just as impressive and interesting! Particularly for this community imo.

Honestly, Justine is just an amazing engineer. If you look at any of her other projects, they are all equally impressive :slightly_smiling_face: .

It seems like her goal is to make APE a viable alternative platform in the way that MUSL has become a mature viable alternative GLIBC.

It's not the sexiest vision for the future of code, but I think it is still an important one.
